question: What does the Latvian idiom '{{lv-LV|Vilkt deķi uz savu pusi||VILKT DE-ki uz SA-vu PU-si}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Act selfishly and think only about your own benefit.
incorrectAnswers: Hand something over very easily and clearly.
Be a little crazy or odd.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday latvian for the idea 'Act selfishly and think only about your own benefit.'. Word-to-word: "to pull the blanket to your own side."

question: What does the Latvian idiom '{{lv-LV|Nolikt kā uz paplātes||NO-likt ka uz pa-PLA-tes}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Hand something over very easily and clearly.
incorrectAnswers: Be a little crazy or odd.
Go quiet or lose your voice.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday latvian for the idea 'Hand something over very easily and clearly.'. Word-to-word: "to place it as on a tray."

question: What does the Latvian idiom '{{lv-LV|Kaut kas nav ar pilnu rubli||KAUT kas nav ar PIL-nu RUB-li}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Be a little crazy or odd.
incorrectAnswers: Go quiet or lose your voice.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday latvian for the idea 'Be a little crazy or odd.'. Word-to-word: "something is not with a full ruble."

question: What does the Latvian idiom '{{lv-LV|Nolikt karoti||NO-likt KA-ro-ti}}' most naturally mean?
incorrectAnswers: Go quiet or lose your voice.
Good things attract attention and hangers-on.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday latvian for the idea 'Die or kick the bucket.'. Word-to-word: "to put down the spoon."

question: What does the Latvian idiom '{{lv-LV|Rīklē iekritis kaķis||RIK-le ie-KRI-tis KA-kis}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Go quiet or lose your voice.

explanation: This idiom is used in everyday latvian for the idea 'Go quiet or lose your voice.'. Word-to-word: "a cat has fallen into the throat."

question: What does the Latvian idiom '{{lv-LV|Taisīt zilus brīnumus||TAI-sit ZI-lus BRI-nu-mus}}' most naturally mean?
incorrectAnswers: Good things attract attention and hangers-on.
Refer to an obvious issue everyone is ignoring.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday latvian for the idea 'Exaggerate and tell wild stories.'. Word-to-word: "to make blue wonders."

question: What does the Latvian idiom '{{lv-LV|Kur ir medus, tur mušas||KUR ir ME-dus tur MU-shas}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Good things attract attention and hangers-on.
incorrectAnswers: Refer to an obvious issue everyone is ignoring.

explanation: This idiom is used in everyday latvian for the idea 'Good things attract attention and hangers-on.'. Word-to-word: "where there is honey, there are flies."

question: What does the Latvian idiom '{{lv-LV|Vēders smējās||VE-ders SME-jas}}' most naturally mean?
incorrectAnswers: Refer to an obvious issue everyone is ignoring.

explanation: This idiom is used in everyday latvian for the idea 'Laugh very hard.'. Word-to-word: "the belly laughed."

explanation: This idiom is used in everyday latvian for the idea 'Refer to an obvious issue everyone is ignoring.'. Word-to-word: "an elephant in the room."

question: What does the Latvian idiom '{{lv-LV|Nav ne āža, ne kazas||NAV ne A-zha ne KA-zas}}' most naturally mean?
